title: SIFMA, FSI Statement on Order Vacating the DOL 2024 Fiduciary Rule and Related PTEs
summary: A U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas vacated the Department of Labor's 2024 Definition of Investment Advice Fiduciary rule and related prohibited transaction exemptions. SIFMA and FSI, as Plaintiffs-Intervenors in the case, praised the ruling as consistent with the Fifth Circuit's 2018 striking down of a similar 2016 DOL rule.
key_points: The U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Texas vacated the DOL's 2024 Retirement Security Rule: Definition of an Investment Advice Fiduciary
The court also vacated Amendment to Prohibited Transaction Exemption 2020-02, Amendment to PTE 84-24, and Amendments to PTEs 75-1, 77-4, 80-83, 83-1, and 86-128
SIFMA and FSI were Plaintiffs-Intervenors in the legal challenge to the 2024 Rule
The court found the 2024 Rule exceeded DOL's statutory authority and was arbitrary and capricious
The court ruled the 2024 Rule was materially indistinguishable from the 2016 DOL rule struck down by the Fifth Circuit in 2018
Plaintiffs argued the rule would have jeopardized investors' access to advice and education by improperly expanding the definition of fiduciary
why_it_matters: This court ruling reinstates the prior regulatory framework for investment advice fiduciary standards in retirement accounts. Broker-dealers and investment advisors can continue operating under existing guidance without the expanded fiduciary obligations that would have been imposed by the 2024 Rule.
market_structure_context: The ruling affects the regulatory framework governing how financial advisors deliver services to retirement account holders. The vacated prohibited transaction exemptions (PTEs) governed conditions under which advisors could receive compensation for advice on retirement accounts. This is regulatory compliance material, not market microstructure or trading operations.

issues_found: Source is a trade association press release (advocacy material), not a primary source. The content represents the plaintiffs' perspective on the court ruling, not the ruling itself.
